summary: DHH recounts his journey from dismissing American patriotic self-image as delusional to genuinely appreciating the First Amendment's value. He argues that recent crackdowns on speech in the UK, EU, and Canada—punishing 'misinformation' and 'hate speech' with harsh sentences and regulatory threats—have vindicated America's constitutional free speech protections. He uses the campy patriotic song 'God Bless the USA' as a framing device, admitting it now rings true to him as a Danish immigrant. His core argument is that America's First Amendment has proven a uniquely resilient bulwark against the new wave of censorship spreading across Western democracies.
key_points: The UK's response to riots includes fast-track tribunals handing out harsh sentences for speech offenses like 'anti-establishment rhetoric' and criticism of migration policy
The EU is using the Digital Markets Act—originally meant to counter tech monopolies—as a tool to pressure platforms into censoring speech
Western democracies that criticized the Patriot Act are now embracing similar crisis-driven authoritarian measures around speech
America's First Amendment stands out as uniquely protective compared to other Western nations' approaches to regulating speech
The right to 'be full of shit' is preferable to any government claiming authority to define truth and falsehood a priori
money_quotes: I used to find the American self-image of being this uniquely freedom-loving, freedom-having people delusional. Turns out I just needed to wait a little longer for the patriotic hymns to begin ringing true.
There's a shockingly draconian crackdown on 'misinformation' and 'hate speech', the two stalwart euphemisms for 'speech we don't like', going on in Britain in particular at the moment.
Weren't The Enlightened Ones supposed to recognize the dangers of using a crisis to push through police-state measures?

America's first amendment, the constitutional right to free speech, including the right to 'misinformation' and even 'hate speech', has proven a surprisingly effective and resilient bulwark against the new rise of censorship and blasphemy laws gaining steam across the Atlantic.
I'd much rather live in a country that embraces everyone's right to BE FULL OF SHIT than one that pretends it can declare a priori what's true and what's false.
I'm not an American. But I sure am becoming a lot more appreciative of the core principles my adopted country was founded on.
The Digital Markets Act, which I've in the past applauded for its attempt to counter tech monopolies, is being wielded as the cudgel for this authoritarian crackdown.
key_insight: Recent speech crackdowns across Western democracies have vindicated America's First Amendment as a uniquely effective protection against government-imposed censorship, even for a skeptical immigrant.
